causal_chain.from: Increased kinetic energy of water molecules (due to heating)
causal_chain.mechanism: Heating water increases the average speed of its molecules. Faster molecules are more likely to escape the liquid phase and enter the gas phase, thus increasing vapor pressure.

causal_chain.to: External pressure on the water surface
causal_chain.mechanism: Atmospheric pressure is the force exerted by the weight of air above a given point. At sea level, this pressure is approximately 1 atmosphere.

causal_chain.from: Vapor pressure of water equals atmospheric pressure at sea level
causal_chain.mechanism: Boiling occurs when the vapor pressure of the liquid equals the surrounding atmospheric pressure, allowing bubbles of vapor to form throughout the liquid and rise to the surface.

causal_chain.to: Water temperature remains at 100 degrees Celsius (at sea level)
causal_chain.mechanism: During boiling, the energy input is used to overcome the intermolecular forces holding the water molecules together in the liquid phase, rather than increasing the temperature. This maintains a constant temperature until all the water has vaporized.

causal_chain.to: Increased kinetic energy of water molecules (due to heating)
causal_chain.mechanism: Heating adds energy to the water molecules, increasing their kinetic energy.
